run.output:
Query returned 1 rows
Execution time: 19 ms

Q6.notes: Result: 1230113636.01. Correct. Performance: 19 ms is at DuckDB performance level (19.99 ms avg), 35% faster than GenDB baseline (29.3 ms). Excellent result for baseline iteration.

Q6.bottleneck: Single-table scan with selective filtering and simple aggregation. Not a bottleneck at current performance level, but optimization potential exists.
Q6.root_cause: The query is already well-optimized for iteration 0: morsel-driven parallelism across 64 cores, minimal synchronization (single reduction), and low-cardinality aggregation (1 group). Current throughput ~3.2M rows/ms. Remaining latency driven by sequential predicate evaluation and lack of SIMD/index-based optimizations.

analysis.overall: Q6 achieves competitive performance (19 ms) matching DuckDB average. The baseline implementation demonstrates effective parallelism and morsel-driven execution. Correctness is verified. Further optimization opportunities exist but current code is already high-quality.

performance_optimizations.description: Utilize lineitem.l_shipdate.zmap (min/max block-level statistics) to skip entire 100K-row blocks where l_shipdate constraints cannot be satisfied. Current code ignores the sorted index and zone map available on l_shipdate. Reading lineitem.l_shipdate.zmap will provide block boundaries (typically 4-5K blocks for 60M rows). For each block, check if max(block) < 9131 or min(block) >= 8766 to skip entire blocks. This can eliminate 5-15% of I/O and CPU work on date filtering.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% reduction in total execution time (1-2 ms). Zone maps on sorted columns typically skip 5-10% of blocks without eliminating any qualifying rows.

performance_optimizations.description: Replace scalar predicate evaluation loop with AVX2 SIMD vectorization. Process 4 doubles in parallel for discount BETWEEN check (4 doubles fit in __m256d). Load 4 quantities in __m256d, compare quantity < 24 in SIMD. Combine results with bitwise AND before materializing qualifying rows. Current loop evaluates predicates sequentially per row; SIMD can process 4 rows in parallel using single instruction chains. Given hardware supports AVX-512 (8x double), this is viable. Target discount/quantity filters which are the final selective gates (combined ~3-4% selectivity after date filters).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-15% reduction in filter evaluation time (1-2 ms). SIMD vectorization typically gives 2-4x speedup on selective filters. Conservative estimate: 10% of 19 ms = 1.9 ms improvement.

performance_optimizations.description: Reorder filter evaluation to apply most selective predicates first. Current code evaluates in order: l_shipdate >= 8766 (86% pass) -> l_shipdate < 9131 (14% pass) -> discount BETWEEN (34% pass) -> quantity < 24 (100% pass). After combined date filters (~12% pass), discount BETWEEN (34% of 12% = 4%) and quantity < 24 should be swapped. Evaluate discount before quantity since discount has lower selectivity in the context of already-filtered rows. Additionally, move date filters to the front (they're already there) and combined early via short-circuit logic. Use branch prediction hints (__builtin_expect) for highly selective predicates.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-5% reduction in predicate evaluation cost (0.5-1 ms). Predicate reordering typically gives modest gains (1-3%) on selective workloads, but combined with branch hints can reach 5% for unpredictable filters.

performance_optimizations.description: Current morsel_size is 100K rows. With 64 threads and ~3.2M effective qualifying rows after filtering, this results in ~100 morsels total. Analyze actual cache behavior: L3 cache = 44 MB, 4 columns × 8 bytes (double/int32) per row = 32 bytes per row. 100K rows = 3.2 MB per morsel, well within L3. Consider reducing morsel size to 50K to increase scheduling granularity and reduce tail latency (balanced load across thread pool), or increasing to 250K to amortize thread spawn overhead. Measure impact of morsel_size in [25K, 50K, 100K, 250K, 500K] range.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 0-5% improvement through better load balancing. Current implementation is already well-tuned, so further optimization is marginal.

performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses madvise(..., MADV_SEQUENTIAL) on all 4 columns. This is good for I/O prefetching. Verify mmap hints are optimal: consider madvise(..., MADV_WILLNEED) to pre-read the entire file into page cache if sufficient memory (4 columns × 229M = 916 MB, well within 376 GB system RAM). However, MADV_SEQUENTIAL should already trigger read-ahead. Alternatively, use madvise(..., MADV_RANDOM) if access patterns are non-sequential (unlikely for this query). Current implementation is already optimized. No change needed unless profiling shows page faults.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 0-2% improvement if MADV_WILLNEED reduces page fault latency. Current MADV_SEQUENTIAL is adequate.

summary: Q6 achieves 19 ms execution time, matching DuckDB performance baseline (19.99 ms avg) and 35% faster than GenDB's initial 29.3 ms. The baseline implementation demonstrates strong fundamentals: correct results, effective parallelism (64 cores), morsel-driven execution, and low-cardinality aggregation. No critical fixes required. Performance optimization opportunities exist (zone map skipping, SIMD filtering, predicate reordering) but are incremental—estimated 5-15% total improvement would bring execution time to 16-18 ms. Current iteration 0 is high-quality and near-optimal for the single-table aggregate workload.
